coredata.dc:title: Highly selective detection of epinephrine by a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yue
Lan, Wei
He, Song
Guo, Xiaoming
Hai, Chengying
Zhao, Xiangyu
Chen, Hengye
Long, Wanjun
She, Yuanbin
Fu, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: Epinephrine (EP) is a catecholamine hormone with a variety of physiological activities. Monitoring the concentration of EP in drugs, food, biological samples and cosmetics is of great significance for their quality control. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensing method was designed for the high-specificity detection of EP based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs). The EP could interact with the fluorescent senor of N-CDs which emits blue fluorescence to produce concentration- dependent fluorescence quenching through the photo-induced electron transfer (PET). The established sensing method has good linearity in the range of 0.5–10 μM with the LOD of 0.15 μM. More importantly, it is highly selective because similar components with phenolic hydroxyl groups or primary amino groups, even norepinephrine (NEP), could not interfere with the detection. This method can provide a low-cost, rapid and simple new way for the detection of EP, and has a good application prospect in point-of-care assay and in situ test.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Epinephrine
Fluorescent sensor
Photo-induced electron transfer

Detection of EP in real samples
Detection mechanism of EP based on N-CDs
Detection of EP in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China: [Grant No. 32122068 , 31972164 , 32001789 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM characterization of the synthesized N-CDs (A); XPS (B), C1s (C), N1s (D), and O1s (E) spectra of the N-CDs; (F) Fluorescence excitation (red line), emission (blue line) spectra and UV absorption spectrum (black line) of the N-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra (A), UV spectra (B) and Mid-infrared spectra (C) of the N-CDs before and after reacting with EP.
(A) Zeta potential characterization of N-CDs before (black line) and after (red line) reaction with EP; High-resolution curve of N1s XPS before (B) and after (C) reaction of N-CDs with EP.

(A) Schematic diagram of the mechanism of interaction between N-CDs and EP; (B) Normalized absorption spectrum of EP and normalized fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs (The crossing point of these two spectra corresponds to the excited-state energy,E00, which were calculated to be 3.55 eV); Cyclic voltammogram curves of N-CDs (C) and EP (D) in 0.1 M KCl solution.

Fluorescence lifetime results of N-CDs before (black line) and after (red line) the reaction with EP (τ1, τ2 and τ3 were fast, medium and slow fluorescence lifetimes, respectively, A1, A2 and A3 were the corresponding weight factors, and τavg was the mean fluorescence lifetime).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (B) Fluorescence intensity changes of the N-CDs in AcOH-water and HCl-water systems with different proportions; (C) The effect of Tris-HCl buffer solution with different pH on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs (black color) and N-CDs + EP (pH = 5: red, pH = 6: green, pH = 7: dark blue, H2O: light blue, pH = 8: purple, pH = 9: yellow); (D) Effect of reaction time on the N-CDs fluorescence intensity. (The concentration of N-CDs was 0.02 mg/mL, and the concentration of EP was 1 × 10-4 M).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs reacted with different concentrations of EP (a-g: 0–10 µM); (B) Linear regression curve between EP concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F1).

Fluorescence spectra (A) and fluorescence intensity histograms and structural formulas (B) of several structurally similar components (1 × 10-4 mol/L) and EP (2.5 × 10-6 mol/L) reacting with N-CDs (UA: uric acid; CA: catechol; LA: L-lactic acid; PEA: β-phenylethylamine; DA: dopamine; NEP: norepinephrine; EP: epinephrine); (C) Effects of several metal ions at concentration of 1 × 10-4 mol/L (Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+) on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs.

ce:simple-para.ce:inf: Gibbs free energy differences (ΔG) for the reaction of each analyte with N-CDs calculated based on the Rehm−Weller equation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The Ered (A) value of N-CDs was −2.10 V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of EP by proposed method in cosmetic toner samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of five replicate measurements ± standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of EP by proposed method in human urine samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of five replicate measurements ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Highly selective detection of epinephrine by a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Univ Technol, Coll Chem Engn, State Key Lab Breeding Base Green Chem Synth Tech, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab Breeding Base Green Chem Synth Tech

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Univ Technol, Coll Chem Engn, State Key Lab Breeding Base Green Chem Synth Tech, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yue Wu and Wei Lan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Epinephrine (EP) is a catecholamine hormone with a variety of physiological activities. Monitoring the concentration of EP in drugs, food, biological samples and cosmetics is of great significance for their quality control. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensing method was designed for the high-specificity detection of EP based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs). The EP could interact with the fluorescent senor of N-CDs which emits blue fluorescence to produce concentration- dependent fluorescence quenching through the photo-induced electron transfer (PET). The established sensing method has good linearity in the range of 0.5–10 μM with the LOD of 0.15 μM. More importantly, it is highly selective because similar components with phenolic hydroxyl groups or primary amino groups, even norepinephrine (NEP), could not interfere with the detection. This method can provide a low-cost, rapid and simple new way for the detection of EP, and has a good application prospect in point-of-care assay and in situ test.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Epinephrine (EP) is a catecholamine hormone secreted by the adrenal medulla, which has the functions of regulating myocardium, increasing blood pressure, activating sympathetic nerves, transporting glucose to muscles, and promoting muscle activity [1]. Many diseases are related to changes in EP concentration in body fluids, such as angina pectoris, hepatitis and cirrhosis, brain injury and other diseases are biologically able to cause changes in EP concentration [2,3]. Determination of EP in urine can determine the localization of adrenal and neural tissue tumors [4,5]. Therefore, the determination of EP in urine has attracted much attention in clinical diagnosis. Furthermore, the addition of EP in cosmetics can prevent skin aging, remove wrinkles, increase skin elasticity and other functions, but excessive use will affect the blood circulation of the central nervous system, heart and kidney and other major organs, resulting in symptoms of ischemia and functional damage of various systems [6]. So, EP testing becomes one of the quality control items of cosmetics. In addition, there are reported that EP has been excessively injected into pigs before slaughter by unscrupulous traders to produce better-looking water-injected meat [7]. In a word, the detection of EP is of great significance in clinical testing, cosmetics and food safety control.

The most commonly used method of EP detection is high performance liquid chromatography and its combined technology [8]. To improve the high requirements of the instrument and high cost of HPLC methods, other rapid detection methods, such as electrochemical analysis [9,10], capillary electrophoresis [11–14], chemiluminescence [15-18] have been successfully developed. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, a variety of nanomaterials (gold nanoparticles, quantum dots, porphyrins nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots, graphene, etc.) have been widely used to construct small portable sensors [19-23]. These sensors have unique advantages of fast response speed, rapid simplicity and high integrability, and are very suitable for on-site screening and real-time determination. They have become the hot spot and frontier of rapid detection technology development [24-29]. Various nanosensors have been successfully developed for the detection of EP, such as graphene quantum dots, carbon dot and CdTe quantum dots nanocomposite, molecular imprinted sensor including graphitic carbon nitride, N-doped carbon quantum dots composite, nanostructured Au electrode [8,30,31]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have become the emerging and more promising nanomaterials for fluorescence sensor construction due to their excellent fluorescence performance, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and many other special properties [32-34]. However, most of established methods were responsive to both EP and norepinephrine, which can interfere with each other when both components are present in the samples. Finding out sensors that only respond to a single component will solve the interference problems.

Based on this, a blue fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) was selected from a variety of carbon spots because its fluorescence can be specifically quenched by epinephrine without interference of norepinephrine and other chemically similar components. The selecting process of carbon quantum dots was described in the Section 1 of Supplementary Material and shown in Fig. S1. Thus, a novel EP detection method was established using the selected N-CDs as a “turn-off” fluorescence sensor. The mechanism of interaction between EP and the N-CDs was explored, and the accuracy and selectivity of the new sensing method were verified by applying this method to complex matrixes. This method can provide a low-cost, rapid and simple new way for the detection of epinephrine, and has a good application prospect in point-of-care assay and in situ test. A graphical representation of the proposed method is illustrated in Graphical Abstract.

Citric acid, ammonium hydroxide, glacial acetic acid, hydrochloric acid, trihydroxymethylaminomethane (Tris), NaOH, FeCl3, CdCl2, HgCl2, AgNO3, CuCl2, ZnSO4, BaCl2, Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Uric acid (UA, C5H4N4O3, 98%), catechol (CA, C6H6O2, 99%), L-lactic acid (LA, C3H6O3, 98%), β-phenylethylamine (PEA, C8H11N, 99%), dopamine(DA, C8H11NO2, 99%), norepinephrine (NEP, C8H11NO3, 98%) and epinephrine (EP, C9H13NO3, 98%) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human serum solutions were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Toner was purchased on the official website of a brand. Ultrapure water was prepared using the Milli-Q purified system (18.25 MΩ). Cosmetic toner samples were purchased from local stores. The urine sample was obtained from a single donor.

The morphologies and sizes of N-CDs were characterized by a Hitachi H-7000FA (Tokyo, Japan) scanning electron microscopy. FT-IR spectra were recorded by a Jascoft-IR420 infrared spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV-1800PC ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000FL fluorescence spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) using 10/10 nm slit width and equipped with an 1 cm quartz cell at room temperature. Zeta potential was measured by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). The characterization of XPS was performed by a Thermo Kalpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Massachusetts, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by an Edinburgh FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

ThesynthesismethodofN-CDswasadjustedaccordingtothereported methods [19]. Briefly, 3 g citric acid was dissolved in 22.5 mL ultrapure water, 1.5 mL ammonia was added, stirred by ultrasonic wave for 2 min, and then heated by microwave on high heat (The power was 750 W and the temperature was 100 ℃) for 5 min to obtain a dark black-brown thick liquid. Then, 20 mL ultra-pure water was added and dissolved completely. A 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane was used to remove insoluble impurities and the filtrate was lyophilized for 16 h to obtain powdered N-CDs, which was redissolved in deionized water for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of EP based on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 100 μL of N-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) was mixed thoroughly with 100 μL of EP at different concentrations and then 800 μL ultrapure water. After 3 min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded with the excitation wavelength at 340 nm. The voltage and slit width were 400 V and 10 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of EP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Using cosmetic toner and human urine as the standard recovery samples, the stability and applicability of the method were discussed. In order to simulate the real use situation, for cosmetic toner samples, we used a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane to filter to remove large particulate impurities, and used it as a solvent to prepare different concentrations of EP spiked solutions for detecting. For human urine samples with very low EP content, the pretreatment method of concentration after marking was adopted to increase the concentration of EP in urine samples and reduce the detection error. Therefore, we took 4 mL urine samples as the solvent, prepared different concentrations of EP spiked solution, added sodium hydroxide (0.05 mol/L, 8 µL) to adjust the pH to 10, added 4 mL ethyl acetate extraction, and then dried, adding hydrochloric acid (0.5 mol/L, 100 µL) to redissolve to 100 μL, completed the sample labeling and concentration pretreatment, to be tested. Then, the above prepared spiked solution was detected by fluorescence spectrum according to the steps in 2.4 to obtain the fluorescence intensity after quenching.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the size and morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the N-CDs are spherical and uniformly distributed with the average particle size is 7.65 nm. These particles showed the obvious crystal lattice with 0.23 nm of lattice space between parallel lines.Then X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) was used to characterize N-CDs surface characteristics, and the XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 1 (B-E). The full scan spectrum (Fig. 1B) specifies the existence of C1s, N1s, and O1s in the synthesized N-CDs. The peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.8 eV, and 531.8 eV in this overall spectrum can be interpreted as the peaks for C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The deconvolution of the C1s spectrum in Fig. 1C shows three peaks at 284.4, 285.5, and 288.8 eV, which are assigned to C–C, C-N/C-O, and C = O, respectively. The fitted three peaks in. Fig. 1D at 400.36 eV, 399.58 eV, and 399.25 eV are attributed to the N–H of the amino group, N-(C)3, and C-N-C groups, respectively. The two peaks in the high resolution energy spectrum of the O1s in Fig. 1E at 530.62 eV and 531.93 eV correspond to the C = O and C-OH groups, respectively [19]. The above TEM and XPS characterization results indicated the successful synthesis of N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs can emit blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Fig. 1F shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, UV absorbance spectrum of N-CDs. The maximum excitation wavelength of N-CDs was 340 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was 420 nm, and the UV absorption peak was located at 335 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of EP based on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A, both EP solutions with concentrations of 1 × 10-4 M and 1 × 10-6 M could significantly quench the fluorescence of N-CDs and slightly redshift the emission peak. Moreover, the degree of quenching and redshift was proportional to the concentration, which proved the feasibility of using carbon dots as fluorescence sensors to detect EP. The UV absorption spectra of the N-CDs before and after adding EP shown in Fig. 2B. The N-CDs has only one characteristic absorption peak at 335 nm, which may be caused by the surface groups. Two characteristic absorption peaks appeared in UV absorption spectra after EP was added. The first peak at 278 nm could be attributed to the characteristic absorption of EP, and the second peak at 325 nm was the slight blue shift of the absorption peak of the N-CDs, which also proved the interaction between EP and the surface groups of the N-CDs.

To further elucidate the interaction between EP and N-CDs, the mid-infrared spectra of the N-CDs and the N-CDs reacted with EP were first investigated and shown in Fig. 2C. The black line is the infrared spectrum of N-CDs. C-O stretching vibration at 1160 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 are attributed to C-OH group. The peak at 1750 cm−1 is attributed to C = O stretching vibration of –COOH and –CONH2 groups. The broad peak at 3200–3450 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration of –COOH and N–H stretching vibration of –CONH2. From the above results, it is inferred that the reactive surface groups of N-CDs are –COOH, –CONH2 and C-OH groups. The red line in Fig. 2C is the infrared spectrum of the N-CDs reacted with EP. It can be seen that the peaks of N-CDs at 1160 cm−1, 1350 cm−1, 3200–3450 cm−1 disappeared, and the peak intensity at 1750 cm−1 weakened significantly, indicating that the corresponding groups (–COOH, –CONH2 and C-OH) were all involved in the reaction.

Furthermore, we characterized the Zeta potential of N-CDs before and after the reaction with EP, and the measurement results are shown in Fig. 3 A. The zeta potential of N-CDs in aqueous solution is −19.3 mV, which has a high negative potential, indicating that not only the carboxyl group but also the hydroxyl group on the surface of N-CDs is ionized. After the addition of EP reaction, the surface potential of N-CDs significantly increased to −6.2 mV. It indicates that the negatively charged carboxyl group and hydroxyl group are involved in the reaction. In addition, XPS N1s high-resolution spectra of N-CDs before and after the reaction with EP showed a new peak at 400.5 eV after the reaction (Fig. 3B and 3C). It is believed that the protonation of N would induce its bonding energy to become higher [35]. This new peak is therefore attributed to the formation of N-containing hydrogen bonds with N atoms providing lone pair electrons bound to H atoms of the carboxyl group and the hydroxyl group. Combined with Zeta potential characterization, it has been proved that –COOH and –OH on the surface of N-CDs are reactive groups, it is deduced that the N atom of the EP side chain –NH- forms a hydrogen bond with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs, which provided the condition for electron transfer. The specific mechanism of action is shown in Fig. 4 A. And since there is no significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of EP and the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs, we exclude internal filtering effect (IFE) and resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanisms (Fig. 4B). Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) is considered to be the predominant mechanism of EP-induced decrease in N-CDs fluorescence [20,36-39]. According to Marcus theory, PET is thermodynamically favorable when the change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) is negative (ΔG less than 0) [40]. To determine the value of ΔG in our system, we employed the Rehm − Weller equation (1) [41]:
(1)
Δ G = e
(
E
OX
( D )
-
E
red
( A )
)
-
E 00
-
e 2
ε a
Where E00 is the excitation energy of the reactants, calculated from the cross point of the normalized absorption and emission spectra. The normalized spectra as shown in Fig. 4B were obtained by using the maximum normalization method to normalize the two curves into the interval [0–1] after the calibration of the minimum values, which were implemented by the software Origin 9.0. Thus, E00 is calculated to be 3.55 eV by the following two equations (2) and (3):
(2)
ν = c / λ
(3)
E 00
= h ν

ν is the frequency, c is the speed of light (c≈3 × 108 m/s), λ is the wavelength at the cross point of the UV absorption spectra and the fluorescence emission spectra in the normalized spectra (λ = 350 nm), and h is the Planck constant (h≈6.626 × 10-34 J·s). e2/εa is the Coulomb interaction energy associated with the solvent, which is negligible in water. Eox (D) is the first one-electron oxidation potential of the electron donor and Ered (A) is the first one-electron reduction potential of the electron acceptor. Both Eox (D) and Ered (A) can be estimated by electrochemical methods. Cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 4C and 4D) show that the Ered (A) of N-CDs is located at −2.10 V, and the Eox (D) of EP is located at + 1.08 V.

Finally, the ΔG of the reaction between EP and N-CDs calculated by equation (1) is − 0.37 eV, indicating that the electron transfer from EP to the excited state N-CDs is thermodynamically allowable. That is, EP and N-CDs participate in the reaction as electron donors and electron acceptors, respectively, during this PET process.

Further, we discussed the changes in the lifetime of N-CDs fluorescence during the reaction. As shown in Fig. 5 , the average fluorescence lifetime (FLT) of N-CDs is 5.9894 ns, which is shortened to 3.4622 ns after reaction with EP, indicating that this PET process is dynamic fluorescence quenching, the collision of the quench EP with the N-CDs fluorophore resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. It further provides strong evidence for the proposed PET quenching mechanism [42].

Combined with the reaction results of structurally similar components of EP between N-CDs shown in selectivity test results in Fig. 8A and 8B, similar components with phenolic hydroxyl groups or primary amino groups, even norepinephrine (NEP), could not affect the fluorescence of N-CDs. Because of the high structural similarity between these analytes and EP, especially the structural difference between NEP and EP is only in their side chain amino groups (secondary amine –NH- for EP and primary amine –NH2 for NEP), we further identified the secondary amine (–NH-) on the EP structure as the main site of action. For further verification, we similarly used the Rehm − Weller equation (1) to calculate the Gibbs free energy (ΔG) of each analyte interacting with N-CDs. The Eox (D) value of each analyte can be obtained from the cyclic voltammogram (Fig. S2). The corresponding excitation energy E00 (Fig. S3) can be calculated from the cross point of the absorption spectrum of each analyte and the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs. Finally, the ΔG of NEP, DA, LA, UA, CA, PEA reacted with N-CDs are all greater than 0 which are listed in Table 1 , indicating that the electron transfer reaction between other analytes and N-CDs could not be carried out spontaneously. The results further indicated that the –NH- methyl substitution of the phenylethylamine side chain is necessary for the interaction with N-CDs in the chemical structure of epinephrine.

In conclusion, the mechanism of EP-induced N-CDs fluorescence quenching is as follows: The N atom of the secondary amine (–NH-) group in the side chain of EP provides a single pair of electrons, forming an intermolecular hydrogen bond with the –COOH and –OH on the surface of N-CDs, which induces the transfer of excited electrons from EP to N-CDs, that is, photoinduced electron transfer effect, and eventually leads to the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs.

To make the detection more sensitive and reliable, several conditions including the fluorescence excitation wavelength, the type and pH of buffer solution, as well as the response time were optimized. Firstly, the fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths were observe whether the fluorescence properties of the N-CDs changed. As can be seen from Fig. 6 A, under different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak of N-CDs in the range of 290–340 nm gradually increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength, while the fluorescence intensity decreases when the wavelength is greater than 340 nm. Therefore, 340 nm was selected as the best excitation wavelength in this experiment, and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is about 875 at this excitation wavelength. Fig. 6B shows the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in acetic acid–water and HCl-water systems with different proportions. It can be seen that the HCl-water system has a greater influence on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Therefore, Tris-HCl buffer is selected as the candidate solvent. Then, the changes of N-CDs fluorescence intensity before and after adding EP were measured in Tris-HCl buffer solution and water with different pH values. The results are shown in Fig. 6C, N-CDs detection of EP is greatly influenced by pH, and ultrapure water has the best effect, so ultrapure water was finally selected as the solvent for subsequent experiments. Then, the response time of the reaction between EP and N-CDs in a detection process also affects the stability of the method. We explored the changes of the fluorescence intensity after adding EP to the N-CDs. As shown as Fig. 6D, 5 min later, the response was complete, fluorescence intensity did not change significantly in 30 min, which proved that the material had good fluorescence stability in a certain period of time. Also, consider 5 min as the optimal reaction time.

With the optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with different levels of EP (0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7 and 10 μM) were measured. From Fig. 7
A, with the increase of EP concentration, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, and the maximum emission wavelength gradually redshifted slightly as well. The fluorescence ratios (F0/F1) were calculated to establish linear relationship (Fig. 7B). The results showed that the F0/F1 had a good linearity to the concentration of EP in the range from 0.5 to 10 μM. The linear fitting equation was y = 0.026[CEP] + 1.142, R2 = 0.993. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.15 μM according to the 3σ rule (3σ/KSV, n = 5). RSD values (n = 5) of F0/F1 measured for each EP concentration were less than 1.0 %, which means that the method has good repeatability and stability.

To investigate the selectivity of N-CDs detection method for EP, several references substances similar to EP were selected, such as catechol, β-phenylethylamine, dopamine and norepinephrine. Two other possible interference compounds, lactic acid and uric acid, were also examined. As shown in Fig. 8A and 8B, N-CDs fluorescence was significantly quenched only in the presence of EP, and no significant changes were observed in the presence of other samples with 40 times concentrations. In addition, eight metal ions (Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+) at 40 times the concentration of EP had almost no effect on the fluorescence of the N-CDs (Fig. 8C). These results indicated that the synthesized N-CDs have good selectivity to EP. Detection of EP using the “turn-off” fluorescent sensor of N-CDs can even be distinguished by norepinephrine, which is the coexisting interference most similar to epinephrine in complex biological matrixes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of EP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above good test results, in order to verify the applicability of the method in practical samples, cosmetic toner and human urine samples were selected for the test. The prepared labeled samples with different concentrations were added to the N-CDs system for testing, and the test results were shown in Table 2 and Table 3 . The spiked recovery of EP in complex samples was in the range of 97.2%∼105.0%, and the relative standard deviation was less than 3.0 %. These results indicated that the fluorescence sensing system may also provide a simple, rapid and accurate method for the detection of EP in biological samples.

A simple and sensitive method for the detection of EP based on N-CDs fluorescence quenching sensor was designed in this study. The newly synthesized water-soluble N-CDs can emit blue fluorescence at 420 nm wavelength, which can be quenched by EP with high selectivity. Experimental and theoretical results have proved that the specific mechanism of its action is PET effect: The secondary amine (–NH-) of the EP side chain forms hydrogen bonds with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs, thereby inducing electron transfer from EP to N-CDs in the excited state, leading to fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. The reaction was highly selective for EP and was not interfered by norepinephrine and other chemically similar components. A linear relationship between EP concentration and fluorescence quenching degree of N-CDs was established and applied successfully to the detection of EP in human urine and cosmetic toner. The new one-step and label-free detection method of EP has the advantages of simple operation, low toxicity, high selectivity and no need of expensive equipment, shows a good prospect in biological, food and drug analysis.

Yue Wu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wei Lan: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Song He: Investigation. Xiaoming Guo: Investigation. Chengying Hai: Formal analysis. Xiangyu Zhao: Visualization. Hengye Chen: Visualization. Wanjun Long: Writing – review & editing. Yuanbin She: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Haiyan Fu: Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China: [Grant No. 32122068, 31972164, 32001789].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122760.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stress reduction strategies in breast cancer: review of pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic based strategies
Semin. Immunopathol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Gosain, E.NGage-Bouchard, C. Ambrosone, E. Repasky, S. Gandhi, Stress reduction strategies in breast cancer: review ofpharmacologicand non-pharmacologicbased strategies, Seminar in Immunopathology 42 (2020) 719-734. 10.1007/s00281-020-00815-y.

Talanta

Anal. Chim. Acta

J. Chromatogr. B
